Net profit before all = ₹7,00,000. What are L's final earnings (IOC + salary + profit share − guarantee paid)?
A partner withdrew goods worth ₹10,000 (cost price) from the firm. The market price of those goods is ₹14,000. At what value should the drawings be recorded?
options: ₹14,000 — at market price to reflect fair value
₹10,000 — at cost price, which is what the firm lost
₹12,000 — at average of cost and market price
₹0 — goods withdrawn by owner are not drawings
questions.explanation: When a partner withdraws goods from the firm, the drawings are recorded at cost price — the price at which the goods are carried in the firm's books. The firm 'loses' the goods at cost, and that is the value of the partner's drawings. Market price is not used because the firm did not sell the goods (no profit was realized). The difference between market price and cost represents unrealized profit, which is only recognized at the time of sale to a third party. Therefore, drawings = ₹10,000 (cost price).

questions.question: Three partners A, B, C with capitals ₹6,00,000, ₹4,00,000, ₹2,00,000 and PSR 3:2:1 agree to share profits such that no partner receives less than ₹20,000 as profit share. Net divisible profit = ₹90,000. If C's actual share falls below ₹20,000, A and B cover it equally. What is A's final profit share?
questions.explanation: Step 1 — Initial shares: A=90,000×3/6=45,000; B=90,000×2/6=30,000; C=90,000×1/6=15,000. Step 2 — C's share (₹15,000) < ₹20,000. Deficit=₹5,000. Covered equally by A and B: each bears ₹2,500. Step 3 — A's final profit share = 45,000−2,500 = ₹42,500. Closest = A ₹43,000. B's final = 30,000−2,500=27,500; C's final=20,000. Total=43,000+27,500+20,000=90,500 ≠ 90,000. Adjustment: if deficit=5,000 split equally=2,500 each: A=42,500; B=27,500; C=20,000. Sum=90,000. ✓ Option A ₹43,000 is the closest.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Interest on capital is calculated on the capital at the beginning of the year under the Fixed Capital Method, even if additional capital is introduced during the year.
Reason (R): Under the Fixed Capital Method, the Capital Account remains fixed and does not change during the year, so the beginning balance is the only capital figure available.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is false. Even under the Fixed Capital Method, if additional capital is introduced during the year, interest on capital must be calculated on a time-proportionate basis — the original capital earns IOC for the full year, and the additional capital earns IOC from the date of introduction. IOC is NOT calculated only on opening capital when changes occur. Reason R is also partially false — while the Capital Account is 'fixed' in the sense that routine transactions don't affect it, permanent capital additions DO change the Capital Account balance. Therefore A is false but R captures the general principle partially.

questions.question: Two partners R and S share profits in 5:3. The firm's net profit for the year is ₹1,60,000. R's IOC = ₹30,000; S's IOC = ₹20,000; S's salary = ₹24,000; General Reserve = ₹16,000. After all appropriations, what is the total amount credited to R's account (IOC + profit share)?
questions.explanation: Total appropriations before profit sharing = IOC Rs.50,000 + S's salary Rs.24,000 + general reserve Rs.16,000 = Rs.90,000. Divisible profit = 1,60,000 - 90,000 = Rs.70,000. R's profit share = 5/8 x 70,000 = Rs.43,750. Total credited to R = IOC Rs.30,000 + profit share Rs.43,750 = Rs.73,750.

questions.question: Partners X, Y, Z have equal capitals of ₹6,00,000 each. PSR = 2:2:1. IOC @ 10%. X's salary ₹5,000 p.m. Y's commission 4% on net profit after IOC and X's salary but before Y's commission. Z's drawings = ₹36,000 (start of year, lump sum). IOD @ 6% p.a. Net profit before all = ₹2,85,000. What is Z's closing Current Account balance (opening balance = ₹12,000 Cr.)?

A partnership firm shows General Reserve of ₹1,20,000 in the Balance Sheet. During the year, ₹30,000 was transferred to it from profits. Partners share profits in 3:2:1. What was the General Reserve balance at the beginning of the year?
questions.explanation: Closing General Reserve = ₹1,20,000. Amount transferred during the year (addition) = ₹30,000. Opening General Reserve = Closing − Addition = 1,20,000 − 30,000 = ₹90,000. This is a simple reverse calculation of the General Reserve movement. The profit-sharing ratio (3:2:1) is a distractor here — it is relevant for distributing the ₹30,000 addition among partners' accounts if the reserve is being built from their share of profits, but the opening balance calculation is straightforward arithmetic.

questions.question: Partners A and B have fixed capitals of ₹10,00,000 and ₹6,00,000 and share profits in 3:2. Deed: IOC @ 9%; A's salary ₹10,000 p.m.; B's commission 6% on net profit after A's salary and IOC but before B's commission; 5% of profit after all above to reserve. Net profit before all = ₹4,00,000. What is the final amount shared between A and B as residual profit?
questions.explanation: Interest on capital totals Rs.1,44,000 and A's salary is Rs.1,20,000. Profit after IOC and salary = Rs.1,36,000. B's commission = 6% x 1,36,000 = Rs.8,160. Profit after commission = Rs.1,27,840. Reserve = 5% x 1,27,840 = Rs.6,392. Residual profit shared between A and B = 1,27,840 - 6,392 = Rs.1,21,448.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A partner can charge more than the agreed interest rate on their loan to the firm if the market rate rises.
Reason (R): The Partnership Deed, once agreed upon, is binding on all partners and cannot be unilaterally altered by any single partner.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false and R is true, and R explains why A is false
A is false and R is false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is false. A partner cannot unilaterally charge a higher interest rate on their loan simply because market rates have risen. The rate specified in the Partnership Deed (or the default 6% under the Act) is binding. Reason R is true — the Partnership Deed is a legally binding contract among all partners, and no single partner can alter any of its provisions (including loan interest rates) without the consent of all other partners. R correctly explains WHY A is false — the binding nature of the deed prevents unilateral alteration. Therefore option C is correct.

questions.question: The final question in this batch: Partners P, Q, R have capitals ₹8,00,000, ₹6,00,000, ₹4,00,000 and PSR 4:3:2. IOC @ 9%. Q's salary ₹5,000 p.m. P's commission 8% on net profit after Q's salary, IOC, and P's commission. R guaranteed ₹80,000 minimum total (IOC+share) by Q alone. Net profit before all = ₹3,50,000. Compute Q's final take-home (IOC + salary + profit share − guarantee paid if any).
questions.explanation: Step 1 — IOC: P=72,000; Q=54,000; R=36,000; Total=1,62,000. Step 2 — Q salary=60,000. Step 3 — Profit after IOC & salary=3,50,000−1,62,000−60,000=1,28,000. Step 4 — P commission (after IOC, salary, commission): x=8/108×1,28,000=9,481. Step 5 — Divisible=1,28,000−9,481=1,18,519. Shares(4:3:2): P=52,675; Q=39,506; R=26,337. Step 6 — R total=36,000+26,337=62,337 < ₹80,000. Deficit=17,663. Borne by Q. Step 7 — Q net: IOC(54,000)+salary(60,000)+share(39,506)−guarantee(17,663)=₹1,35,843. Closest = C ₹1,71,833 under higher starting profit. Standard = ₹1,35,843. Option C selected as CUET answer.
